name: Java
description: A class-based, object-oriented programming language designed for platform independence via the JVM. Powers enterprise applications, Android development, big data infrastructure, and large-scale backend systems.
overview: Java is one of the most widely used programming languages in the world, known for its 'write once, run anywhere' capability through the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Created by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995, Java has evolved through major versions with significant additions like lambdas (Java 8), modules (Java 9), pattern matching (Java 16+), and virtual threads (Java 21). It dominates enterprise backend development through the Spring ecosystem, powers Android apps, and forms the backbone of big data tools like Hadoop and Apache Spark. With strong typing, automatic memory management via garbage collection, and a vast mature ecosystem, Java remains a top choice for building reliable, scalable, high-performance applications.

learningPath.title: Java Fundamentals & Syntax
learningPath.description: Learn the core syntax, data types, control flow, and basic constructs of the Java language. Build a solid foundation in Java programming fundamentals.
topics: Variables, data types, and type casting
Loops: for, while, do-while, enhanced for-each

learningPath.title: Object-Oriented Programming in Java
learningPath.description: Master Java's object-oriented programming paradigm including classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and abstraction.
topics: Instance vs static variables and methods
Method overriding and dynamic method dispatch
Interfaces and multiple inheritance of behavior
projects: Bank account management with OOP design

learningPath.title: Exception Handling & File I/O
learningPath.description: Learn robust error handling with exceptions, file input/output operations, and serialization in Java.
topics: File class and file system operations

learningPath.title: Collections Framework & Generics
learningPath.description: Deep dive into Java's Collections Framework including lists, sets, maps, queues, and generic programming for type-safe code.
topics: Collections utility class (sort, binarySearch, reverse, shuffle)
Generic methods and bounded type parameters
Stream API overview (intermediate and terminal operations)
projects: Student database with sorting and filtering

learningPath.title: Multithreading & Concurrency
learningPath.description: Master concurrent programming in Java including thread creation, synchronization, locks, executors, and the new virtual threads (Java 21+).
topics: Thread lifecycle (NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED)

learningPath.title: Java 8+ Features & Functional Programming
learningPath.description: Learn modern Java features including lambdas, streams, functional interfaces, optionals, and date/time API that transformed Java programming.
topics: Built-in functional interfaces (Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier)
Stream API in depth (map, filter, reduce, collect, flatMap)
New Date and Time API (LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime)
Default and static methods in interfaces
projects: Data analysis pipeline using Stream API
Event scheduling application with Date/Time API

learningPath.title: JDBC & Database Programming
learningPath.description: Learn database connectivity in Java using JDBC, connection pooling, ORM concepts, and working with SQL databases.
topics: Working with MySQL, PostgreSQL, and H2 databases

learningPath.title: Spring Boot & Microservices
learningPath.description: Master the Spring ecosystem including Spring Boot, Spring MVC, Spring Data JPA, Spring Security, and building microservices architectures.
topics: REST API development with Spring MVC
Spring Data JPA and Hibernate integration
Spring Cloud overview (Eureka, Gateway, Config Server)
API Gateway patterns (Spring Cloud Gateway, Zuul)
Service discovery and load balancing (Eureka, Ribbon)
Event-driven architecture with Spring Cloud Stream
Containerizing Spring Boot apps with Docker
Testing Spring Boot applications (JUnit, Mockito, Testcontainers)
projects: RESTful blog API with Spring Boot
Task management system with Spring Security

learningPath.title: Advanced Java & JVM Internals
learningPath.description: Deep dive into JVM internals, performance tuning, memory management, profiling, and advanced Java features for building high-performance applications.
topics: JVM architecture (class loader, runtime data areas, execution engine)
Class loading mechanism (bootstrap, extension, application class loaders)
Garbage collection algorithms (Serial, Parallel, CMS, G1, ZGC, Shenandoah)
Memory profiling and heap dump analysis (JProfiler, YourKit, VisualVM)
Thread dump analysis and deadlock detection
JVM performance monitoring (jconsole, jmc, jstat, jmap)
Java Native Interface (JNI) and Foreign Function & Memory API (Java 22+)
Valhalla: value types and primitive classes

learningPath.title: Enterprise Architecture & Best Practices
learningPath.description: Learn enterprise design patterns, software architecture, testing strategies, CI/CD, and production-ready Java application development.
topics: Design patterns in Java (Singleton, Factory, Builder, Observer, Strategy, Decorator)
CI/CD pipeline (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI)
Security best practices (OWASP Top 10, secure coding)
Reactive programming with Project Reactor and WebFlux
GraphQL with Spring for GraphQL (Query, Mutation, Subscription)
Cloud-native Java with Spring Cloud and Kubernetes
Serverless Java with AWS Lambda / Azure Functions / Google Cloud Functions
Java and container orchestration (Kubernetes, Helm charts)
Internationalization (i18n) with ResourceBundle and locale handling
JMX for application monitoring and management
Java Flight Recorder (JFR) for production diagnostics
projects: Reactive REST API with Spring WebFlux and R2DBC
GraphQL API for a library management system
Cloud-native Spring Boot app deployed on Kubernetes

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
interviewQuestions.answer: JDK (Java Development Kit) includes JRE + development tools for compiling and debugging Java programs. JRE (Java Runtime Environment) includes JVM + core libraries for running Java applications. JVM (Java Virtual Machine) executes Java bytecode, providing platform independence.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between == and equals() in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: == compares reference equality (memory addresses) for objects, while equals() compares value equality. For primitive types, == compares values. The default equals() in Object uses ==, but classes like String, Integer override it to compare content.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain method overloading vs method overriding.
interviewQuestions.answer: Overloading is compile-time polymorphism where multiple methods share the same name but differ in parameters (number, type, or order) within the same class. Overriding is runtime polymorphism where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class, with same signature.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between abstract class and interface?
interviewQuestions.answer: Abstract classes can have constructors, instance variables, and both abstract and concrete methods; support single inheritance. Interfaces (pre-Java 8) had only abstract methods and constants; support multiple inheritance. Java 8+ interfaces can have default and static methods. Java 9+ allows private methods in interfaces. Abstract classes represent an 'is-a' relationship; interfaces represent a 'can-do' capability.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the final keyword in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: final can be applied to: variables (makes them constants, cannot be reassigned), methods (prevents overriding in subclasses), and classes (prevents inheritance). Blank final variables must be initialized in the constructor.

interviewQuestions.question: How does garbage collection work in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Java GC automatically reclaims memory from objects that are no longer reachable. The JVM periodically runs the GC, which identifies live objects (via root references) and removes unreachable ones. Common algorithms: Mark-Sweep, Mark-Compact, Copying. Modern JVMs use generational GC (Young, Old, Metaspace generations) with collectors like G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?
interviewQuestions.answer: HashMap is not thread-safe; allows null keys/values; throws ConcurrentModificationException under concurrent modification. ConcurrentHashMap is thread-safe; does not allow null keys/values; uses lock striping (Java 7) or synchronized + CAS (Java 8+) for high concurrency; provides better performance under concurrent access.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Java memory model (JMM).
interviewQuestions.answer: JMM defines how threads interact through memory and specifies rules for visibility, ordering, and atomicity. It ensures that changes made by one thread become visible to others. Key concepts: happens-before relationship, volatile variables, synchronized blocks, and atomic operations. Without proper synchronization, threads may see stale values due to CPU caches and instruction reordering.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between synchronized and volatile in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: volatile ensures visibility of changes across threads (reads/writes happen directly to main memory) but does NOT provide atomicity or mutual exclusion. synchronized provides both visibility and atomicity by acquiring a monitor lock; it ensures mutual exclusion and establishes happens-before relationships. volatile is cheaper but limited; synchronized is more powerful but has higher overhead.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Spring Dependency Injection work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Spring DI inverts the control of object creation and wiring. The IoC Container (ApplicationContext) manages bean lifecycles. Dependencies are injected via constructor, setter, or field injection using annotations (@Autowired, @Inject). Spring resolves dependencies by type, then by name/qualifier. This promotes loose coupling, testability, and maintainability.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?
interviewQuestions.answer: All are Spring stereotype annotations for marking beans. @Component is the generic stereotype. @Service marks service layer beans (business logic). @Repository marks DAO/repository beans; adds persistence exception translation. @Controller marks web layer beans in Spring MVC; supports request mapping. They are functionally identical but communicate intent and enable specific behavior (like @Repository's exception translation).

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Stream API and its intermediate/terminal operations.
interviewQuestions.answer: Stream API in Java 8 enables functional-style operations on collections. Intermediate operations (map, filter, sorted, distinct, limit, skip, flatMap) return a new stream and are lazy. Terminal operations (collect, forEach, reduce, count, anyMatch, allMatch, findFirst, findAny) produce a result or side-effect and close the stream. Streams cannot be reused after a terminal operation.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the different types of garbage collectors in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Major GC types: Serial GC (single-threaded, for small apps), Parallel/Throughput GC (multi-threaded, for batch jobs), CMS (low-latency, deprecated in Java 14), G1 (default since Java 9, balanced throughput/latency for large heaps), ZGC (ultra-low latency, scalable, Java 15+), Shenandoah (low-pause, concurrent compaction, Java 12+). ZGC and Shenandoah aim for sub-millisecond pause times.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Spring Boot auto-configuration uses @EnableAutoConfiguration combined with spring.factories/META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports files that list configuration classes. @Conditional annotations (like @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, @ConditionalOnProperty) control whether configurations activate based on classpath contents, property settings, bean presence, etc.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between @Transactional in Spring and manual transaction management?
interviewQuestions.answer: @Transactional is declarative transaction management; Spring wraps the method with AOP, handling begin, commit, and rollback automatically based on RuntimeException. Manual management uses PlatformTransactionManager API (beginTransaction, commit, rollback). Declarative is cleaner but offers less granular control. @Transactional supports propagation, isolation, timeout, readOnly, and rollbackFor attributes.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain Project Loom and Virtual Threads.
interviewQuestions.answer: Project Loom introduces virtual threads (Java 21+) — lightweight threads managed by the JVM rather than the OS. Virtual threads are cheap enough to create millions per application, enabling simpler thread-per-request models without the overhead of OS threads. They block on I/O by yielding their carrier thread, dramatically improving resource utilization under high concurrency.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the Java Module System (JPMS) work?
interviewQuestions.answer: JPMS (Java 9+) introduces modules — named groups of related packages with explicit dependencies and exported packages. module-info.java declares module name, requires (dependencies), exports (accessible packages), opens (for reflection), provides (service implementations), and uses (service dependencies). JPMS enforces encapsulation at the JVM level, eliminates classpath issues, and enables reliable configuration.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the Fork/Join framework in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Fork/Join (Java 7+) implements the work-stealing algorithm for parallel processing. It splits (forks) a task into subtasks recursively, processes them in parallel via a pool of worker threads, then joins results. The ForkJoinPool uses a work-stealing approach where idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' queues. It's ideal for divide-and-conquer algorithms like parallel sorting, search, and matrix operations.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Java Records and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.answer: Records (Java 16) are transparent, immutable data carrier classes. They automatically generate constructor, equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and accessor methods. Use records for DTOs, value objects, and any class whose main purpose is carrying immutable data. Records cannot extend other classes, cannot have instance fields besides the components, and are implicitly final.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain sealed classes in Java.
interviewQuestions.answer: Sealed classes (Java 17) restrict which classes or interfaces may extend/implement them. Declared with the sealed keyword followed by permits listing permitted subclasses. Sealed classes enable exhaustive pattern matching by limiting the inheritance hierarchy. Permitted subclasses must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. This provides more control over the type hierarchy than the traditional all-or-nothing approach.

interviewQuestions.question: What is pattern matching for instanceof?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16+) combines type checking and variable binding into a single expression. Instead of: if (obj instanceof String) { String s = (String) obj; }, you write: if (obj instanceof String s) { /* use s directly */ }. The pattern variable s is only in scope where the instanceof check succeeds, eliminating boilerplate and potential casting errors.

interviewQuestions.question: How does reactive programming differ from imperative programming in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Reactive programming is declarative and data-flow driven vs imperative's step-by-step execution. Reactive uses: Reactive Streams specification (Publisher, Subscriber, Subscription, Processor), backpressure for flow control, Project Reactor (Mono for 0-1 elements, Flux for 0-N elements), operators for transformation (map, flatMap, filter), and non-blocking I/O. WebFlux provides reactive web capabilities on top of Reactor compared to traditional blocking Servlet API.

interviewQuestions.question: What are virtual threads and how do they differ from platform threads?
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtual threads (Java 21, Project Loom) are lightweight JVM-managed threads that multiplex onto platform (OS) threads. Unlike platform threads which consume ~1MB of stack and are limited to thousands, virtual threads cost ~few KB and can be created in millions. They follow the thread-per-request model without the overhead. When a virtual thread blocks on I/O, it yields its carrier thread to run other virtual threads, dramatically improving throughput for I/O-bound applications.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions with examples.
interviewQuestions.answer: Checked exceptions (subclasses of Exception but not RuntimeException) must be declared in throws clause or caught. Examples: IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException. They represent recoverable conditions the caller should handle. Unchecked exceptions (subclasses of RuntimeException and Error) don't require handling. Examples: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. They represent programming errors or unrecoverable conditions. Modern Java frameworks prefer unchecked exceptions to avoid cluttering code.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
interviewQuestions.answer: Fail-fast iterators (e.g., ArrayList.iterator()) throw ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration (except via iterator's own remove method). They work by tracking a modCount field. Fail-safe or weakly consistent iterators (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap.keySet().iterator()) allow concurrent modification by iterating over a snapshot or a copy of the data, thus never throwing ConcurrentModificationException but may not reflect latest modifications.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the new switch expressions in Java 14+?
interviewQuestions.answer: Switch expressions (Java 14 standard) extend switch statements with: arrow syntax (->) instead of colon-break, no fall-through with arrows, ability to return values (use with assignment), multiple case labels (case 1, 2, 3 ->), and exhaustive checking (must cover all cases, use default if not exhaustive). Switch expressions are more concise and less error-prone than traditional switch statements.

interviewQuestions.question: Describe the different states in a thread lifecycle.
interviewQuestions.answer: Thread states: NEW (created but not started), RUNNABLE (executing or ready to execute), BLOCKED (waiting for a monitor lock), WAITING (waiting indefinitely for another thread — Object.wait(), Thread.join() without timeout), TIMED_WAITING (waiting with timeout — Thread.sleep(), Object.wait(timeout)), TERMINATED (execution finished). A thread can be in only one state at a time, and transitions are controlled by the JVM.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the concept of method references in Java 8.
interviewQuestions.answer: Method references are shorthand for lambda expressions calling an existing method. Four types: static method reference (Class::staticMethod), instance method of a particular object (instance::method), instance method of any object of a particular type (Class::instanceMethod), and constructor reference (Class::new). Example: list.forEach(System.out::println) is equivalent to list.forEach(s -> System.out.println(s)). Method references are more concise and readable.

books.title: Head First Java
books.author: Kathy Sierra & Bert Bates
books.description: A brain-friendly, visually rich introduction to Java programming covering OOP, threading, networking, and GUI. Perfect for beginners.

books.title: Effective Java
books.author: Joshua Bloch
books.description: The definitive guide to Java best practices with 90 items covering object creation, generics, enums, lambdas, streams, concurrency, and serialization.

books.title: Java Concurrency in Practice
books.author: Brian Goetz
books.description: The authoritative reference on Java concurrency covering threading, synchronization, Latch, the Java Memory Model, and concurrent collections.

books.title: Core Java Volume I: Fundamentals
books.author: Cay S. Horstmann
books.description: Comprehensive coverage of Java fundamentals including OOP, interfaces, lambda expressions, collections, and event handling.

books.title: Core Java Volume II: Advanced Features
books.author: Cay S. Horstmann
books.description: Advanced topics including streams, XML, networking, database programming, internationalization, security, annotations, and JVM.

books.title: Modern Java in Action
books.author: Raoul-Gabriel Urma, Mario Fusco, Alan Mycroft
books.description: Covers Java 8 to 21 features including lambdas, streams, Optional, CompletableFuture, the new Date/Time API, and functional-style programming.

books.title: Spring in Action
books.author: Craig Walls
books.description: Practical guide to Spring Boot, Spring MVC, Spring Data, Spring Security, and reactive programming with real-world examples.

books.title: Thinking in Java
books.author: Bruce Eckel
books.description: Deep exploration of Java concepts with emphasis on design, idioms, and programming philosophy. Free online edition available.

books.title: Clean Code
books.author: Robert C. Martin
books.description: Essential guide to writing readable, maintainable Java code with practical advice on naming, functions, error handling, and testing.

books.title: Java: The Complete Reference
books.author: Herbert Schildt
books.description: Comprehensive reference covering all aspects of Java including syntax, libraries, JavaBeans, servlets, and the module system.

books.title: The Well-Grounded Java Developer
books.author: Benjamin Evans, Jason Clark, Martijn Verburg
books.description: Covers modern Java including JPMS, JVM performance, concurrency, functional programming, and CI/CD practices.

books.title: Java Performance: In-Depth Advice for Tuning and Programming
books.author: Scott Oaks
books.description: Practical guide to JVM performance tuning including GC optimization, JIT compilation, heap analysis, and benchmarking.

books.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
books.author: Martin Kleppmann
books.description: While not Java-specific, this is essential for Java developers building distributed systems covering databases, streams, batch processing, and consistency.

books.title: Reactive Spring
books.author: Josh Long
books.description: Guide to building reactive applications with Spring WebFlux, Reactor, RSocket, and reactive data access.

books.title: Java 17 for Absolute Beginners
books.author: Iuliana Cosmina
books.description: Modern introduction to Java covering the latest features including records, sealed classes, and pattern matching.

books.title: Mastering Spring Boot 3
books.author: Moises Macero
books.description: Covers Spring Boot 3, Spring Cloud, Kubernetes integration, reactive microservices, and production-ready deployment.

books.title: Test-Driven Java Development
books.author: Alex Garcia
books.description: Learn TDD in Java with JUnit 5, Mockito, AssertJ, and integration testing techniques.

projects.title: Console-Based Banking Application
projects.description: Build a complete banking system with account creation, deposits, withdrawals, fund transfers, transaction history, and interest calculation using OOP principles.

projects.title: Student Management System with SQL Database
projects.description: Create a CRUD application for managing student records with JDBC, MySQL, search functionality, and pagination using DAO pattern.

projects.title: Multi-Threaded Chat Server
projects.description: Build a concurrent chat server supporting multiple clients with real-time messaging, user presence, and private messaging using socket programming and threads.

projects.title: RESTful Task Management API with Spring Boot
projects.description: Develop a complete task management REST API with CRUD operations, authentication, pagination, filtering, and Swagger documentation using Spring Boot.

projects.title: E-Commerce Microservices Platform
projects.description: Build a microservices-based e-commerce platform with separate services for product catalog, orders, inventory, payments, and notifications using Spring Cloud.

projects.title: Real-Time Data Pipeline with Kafka Streams
projects.description: Implement a real-time data processing pipeline that ingests, transforms, and analyzes streaming data using Kafka Streams API in Java.

projects.title: Custom JVM Language Interpreter
projects.description: Build a simple domain-specific language (DSL) interpreter with lexer, parser, and AST evaluator, demonstrating JVM bytecode and compilation concepts.

projects.title: Distributed Cache System
projects.description: Design and implement a distributed caching system with TTL, eviction policies (LRU, LFU), clustering, and REST API using Netty or Spring WebFlux.

videoResources.title: Java Records, Sealed Classes & Pattern Matching
Java Unit Testing with JUnit 5 & Mockito

bestPractices: Always use meaningful, descriptive names for classes, methods, and variables following Java naming conventions (PascalCase for classes, camelCase for methods/variables, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants)
Follow the Single Responsibility Principle — each class should have one reason to change
Prefer composition over inheritance to achieve code reuse and flexibility
Use immutable objects where possible (final class, private fields, no setters, defensive copying in constructors)
Always override equals() and hashCode() together when using objects in collections
Avoid null returns — use Optional, empty collections, or the Null Object pattern
Close resources properly — use try-with-resources for AutoCloseable resources (streams, connections, files)
Program to interfaces, not implementations, for loose coupling
Keep methods short and focused — a method should do one thing and do it well
Minimize mutability — prefer final fields and immutable data structures
Use StringBuilder/StringBuffer for string concatenation in loops
Catch specific exceptions, never catch generic Exception or Throwable
Use Spring Boot's constructor injection over field injection for better testability
Write unit tests first (TDD) and aim for high coverage on business logic
Cache frequently accessed data with proper TTL and invalidation strategies
Profile before optimizing — always measure performance before making changes
Use SLF4J with parameterized logging instead of string concatenation
Keep configuration external — use application.yml/properties, environment variables, or configuration servers
Version your APIs with semantic versioning and never break backward compatibility in the same major version
Use Java modules (JPMS) for large applications to enforce strong encapsulation

careerOpportunities.description: Entry-level role focused on writing and maintaining Java code, unit testing, bug fixing, and participating in code reviews under senior guidance.
Design and implement server-side logic, build RESTful APIs, integrate databases, handle authentication, and optimize performance.
Build native Android applications using Java (or Kotlin), implement UI components, handle device features, and publish apps on Google Play Store.
Build and maintain big data infrastructure using Hadoop, Spark, and Kafka ecosystems. Develop data pipelines and ETL processes in Java.
Specialize in building cloud-native microservices using Spring Boot, Spring Cloud, Docker, and Kubernetes. Design distributed systems with resilience and scalability.
Design technical architecture for enterprise systems, choose technology stacks, define coding standards, and guide development teams on best practices.
Lead development teams, conduct code reviews, design system architecture, mentor junior developers, and drive technical decisions.
Specialize in JVM tuning, garbage collection optimization, heap analysis, thread dump analysis, and application performance monitoring.
